metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default retry ceiling in AC3 (e.g., 'max 3 re-batch attempts per combined run before escalating to serial fallback') so the executor has an actionable number.
findings.finding: The retry bound for 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' is unspecified — the executor must choose a number without guidance, which is a real design decision, not a defaultable convention.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Bounded retries: at most N re-batch attempts per batch run (suggested default: 3) before the run is abandoned and all stacks are returned to their agents individually.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a Grep/Read probe) that the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract is implemented in source before this story begins; if it is not yet landed, explicitly list the predecessor story as a hard dependency and name the interface file/module this story must conform to.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract' as if that contract is an already-implemented, reusable interface — but no implementation of this contract (no enum, no handler, no state machine, no module) exists anywhere in the codebase. All grep hits for `needs_rebase` and `ci_failed` land exclusively in plan-review cache files (prior reviews of sibling stories in this same epic), not in any source file.
findings.location: AC2: 'Hand the culprit stack back to its owning agent (reuse the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends on: [serial-failure-path story]' link and reference the specific module (e.g., `outcome.py`, `failure_handler.py`) where the contract lives; if that story is still in flight, block this story on its completion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retry-bounding mechanism concretely in the plan body: specify the numeric bound, what resets the counter (successful landing vs. manual intervention), and what happens at the limit (dead-letter state, alert, park the stack).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' as an accepted AC, presupposing a retry-bounding mechanism exists or is fully specified — but no such mechanism (no retry counter, no max-retry constant, no backoff logic, no dead-letter state) appears anywhere in the plan body or in the codebase.
findings.location: AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'The autolander maintains a per-stack retry counter (max N=3); on the Nth failure the stack is parked (Autosubmit removed, outcome=`ci_failed_permanent`) and not re-queued until an agent re-votes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify (by ticket ID or file path) the prior story that defines the `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract and add a concrete cross-reference in AC2 so the implementer has a grounded interface to reuse.
findings.finding: The plan reuses 'the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract' (AC2) as an existing, stable interface, but no such contract — no module, event schema, interface definition, or enum — exists anywhere in the live source tree. Every codebase occurrence of `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` is in plan-review cache files, not in production code. If the contract is not yet landed, this story cannot bind to it, and the implementer has no grounded interface to reuse.
findings.location: AC2: 'Hand the culprit stack back to its owning agent (reuse the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `gerrit-verify.yaml` (and the g2p dispatch layer) as affected artifacts in the plan, and specify the mechanism by which an isolated CI run is triggered on the culprit stack's change-id to produce the `Verified -1` vote.
findings.finding: AC1 specifies 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct `Verified -1`', but the live CI dispatch infrastructure — `gerrit-verify.yaml` (triggered per change-id via `workflow_dispatch`) and the g2p `gerrit_to_platform.ini` event dispatch layer — is not enumerated as an affected or updated artifact. Triggering an isolated CI run on the culprit stack requires either a new `workflow_dispatch` call carrying the culprit's change-id/patchset, or a synthetic `comment-added` event, neither of which is described. These are real, confirmed consumers outside the (unnamed) artifact's directory.
findings.location: AC1: 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct `Verified -1`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the culprit-attribution mechanism explicitly (e.g., binary-search over stack subsets with isolated CI re-runs, or per-stack parallel isolation runs at batch time) so that implementers have a complete, unambiguous algorithm for decomposing a combined-batch failure into a per-stack cause.
findings.finding: The mechanism for identifying which stack caused a combined-batch CI failure is entirely absent. AC1 states 'attribute the culprit at STACK granularity' but provides no mechanism for how a combined CI failure signal is decomposed into a per-stack cause — e.g., whether this is done via bisection (isolating subsets and re-running CI), by parsing CI failure output for stack-identifying markers, or by running each stack's CI independently in parallel within the batch. Without this step, the rest of the mechanism (handing back the culprit, re-batching innocents) cannot execute correctly.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the bounded-retry policy concretely: the maximum retry count (per-stack or per-batch), where the counter is stored (durable/persisted vs. in-memory), and the exhaustion policy (e.g., fail the stack permanently with `ci_failed`, escalate, or drop to serial).
findings.finding: AC3 states 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' but does not specify the retry bound (count, per-stack vs. per-batch), how retry state is persisted, or what happens when the bound is exhausted. This is a load-bearing safety property — without a concrete bound and exhaustion policy, the thrash-prevention guarantee is unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the concrete CI re-trigger mechanism for an isolated culprit-stack re-run: confirm whether it can reuse the existing gerrit-to-platform `patchset-created` flow (and if so, how without minting a new patchset), or whether a separate GHA dispatch event must be added — and capture this as a constraint or dependency on the CI pipeline stories.
findings.finding: AC1 asserts 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct Verified -1,' but the existing CI trigger path (ADR-0023, ADR-0022) is driven by Gerrit's `patchset-created` event via gerrit-to-platform (g2p). The plan does not verify that this infrastructure supports an ad-hoc, out-of-band CI trigger for an arbitrary subset of in-flight changes without minting a new patchset — which would change the change's revision history and interact with the FFO submit requirement (ADR-0040) and vote copy conditions (ADR-0020/0041) in potentially unintended ways.
findings.location: AC1: 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct Verified -1'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or cross-reference by file path + ticket ID) the `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome enum's full value vocabulary and the decision rules that map CI failure signals to each value, so AC2's 'reuse' claim has a grounded interface for the implementer.
findings.finding: The `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract is referenced in AC2 as a reusable existing interface ('reuse the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract'), but no definition of this enum — no schema, no module, no enum declaration — exists anywhere in the production codebase. Every codebase hit for these strings lands exclusively in `.rebar/cache/plan-review/` JSON files (prior plan review outputs). The enum's value vocabulary and its decision rules are therefore undefined for this plan.
findings.location: AC2: 'reuse the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` outcome contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Contract' subsection to the plan (or link to the predecessor story's output artifact) that enumerates: the outcome enum values, the Gerrit/CI signals that trigger each value, and the data fields passed back to the owning agent. Mark the predecessor story an explicit hard dependency in `deps`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the culprit-attribution decision rule in the plan body: what CI signal (failed job name, changed-file intersection, Gerrit topic tag, etc.) is used to identify the culprit stack, and what the fall-through is when attribution is ambiguous.
findings.finding: The culprit-attribution decision rule — how a combined-batch CI failure is mapped to a specific stack at STACK granularity — is asserted as a behavior in AC1 ('attribute the culprit at STACK granularity') but never specified. No algorithm, signal source, or protocol is described in Why, What, Scope, or ACs. The plan leaves the executor with no grounded method to determine which stack caused the failure.
findings.location: AC1: 'attribute the culprit at STACK granularity (never evict a change from within a stack); re-run CI on the isolated culprit stack'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan: the numeric retry bound, the component that owns the counter (batch orchestrator vs. agent), the storage mechanism (in-memory, persistent store, Gerrit tag), the backoff policy, and the terminal give-up state/action when the bound is reached.
findings.finding: The 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' acceptance criterion (AC3) names a retry-bounding constraint but neither defines the bound (no count, no backoff policy) nor specifies which component owns the retry counter and where that state is persisted. This is an ambiguous counter/state increment with undefined placement.
findings.location: AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or integration-test scenario for each AC (e.g., a scripted combined-batch run with an injected failure that asserts: culprit stack receives Verified -1, innocent stacks proceed to a fresh combined run, and no change lands partially).
findings.finding: No proving commands or verification steps exist for any of the three ACs; an executing agent cannot produce evidence of completion for AC1, AC2, or AC3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all three ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Verification' section with: (1) a scripted or CI-driven test that injects a failure into one stack of a combined batch and asserts `Verified -1` on only that stack; (2) asserts the remaining stacks are re-batched (observable via run log or event count); (3) asserts no partial land (all-or-nothing land check on the innocent batch).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what 'bounded retries' means concretely: specify the retry cap (e.g. N re-batch attempts per original batch), how the count is tracked, and what observable event (log/metric/hard stop) confirms the bound is enforced.
findings.finding: The AC 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' is a non-deferred correctness guarantee but has no end-state-observable proof: the plan never defines what bound applies, how retry counts are tracked, or what observable signal (log entry, metric, hard stop) confirms the bound is enforced.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Re-batching of innocent stacks is retried at most N times per original combined run (N to be defined, e.g. 3); the orchestrator records the attempt count and emits a circuit-break event / moves stacks to a holding queue when the cap is reached.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism to an AC: for example, a named integration test scenario (combined run of N stacks fails on stack K; assert stacks 1..K-1, K+1..N proceed to merge), or an operational signal (orchestrator log entry confirming re-batch was issued for innocent stacks).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion carries a concrete validation mechanism — no before/after workflow comparison, no operational metric target, no dogfooding scenario — making it impossible to confirm the feature delivers observable value versus merely existing in code.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED fixture or spike that exercises the combined-batch → culprit-stack attribution path with synthetic CI failure payloads, covering at least: single-stack failure, multi-stack ambiguous failure, and all-pass (no culprit) scenarios.
findings.finding: The culprit-stack attribution algorithm (how a combined-batch CI failure is mapped back to a specific stack) is asserted to work correctly but is never validated by a spike, RED fixture, or prototype. This is a non-trivial integration with CI output parsing / signal routing whose correctness cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.location: AC line 1 — 'attribute the culprit at STACK granularity … re-run CI on the isolated culprit stack'
findings.suggested_fix: Write a parameterised unit/integration test fixture that feeds representative CI failure outputs (mocked) into the attribution function and asserts the correct culprit stack is identified before building out the full re-batch path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete retry bound (e.g., max N re-runs), backoff strategy, and a terminal state (e.g., park culprit stack in `ci_failed_permanently`, unblock innocents) so bounded retries are enforced, not aspirational.
findings.finding: The 'bounded retries prevent thrash' AC is under-specified: no retry count, no backoff strategy, no dead-letter/give-up state is named. A stack that repeatedly fails will thrash indefinitely in practice if the bound is never defined, and a CI system that is slow or unavailable during the culprit re-run has no circuit-breaker or fallback path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Culprit stack re-runs are capped at N attempts (e.g., 3); on exhaustion the stack is placed in ci_failed state and innocents proceed regardless. CI unavailability during re-run triggers a backoff-and-retry with a circuit-breaker after M consecutive timeouts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a description in the What/Scope section of the retry-bounding mechanism (e.g., max retry count per stack per batch window, or per-cycle limit) so AC3's 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' sub-criterion maps to a concrete, verifiable deliverable.
findings.finding: AC3 introduces 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn' as a verifiable acceptance criterion, but the plan body (Why, What, Scope) contains no description of the retry-bounding mechanism, retry limit design, or churn-handling logic. This is an orphan criterion with no corresponding described deliverable, leaving implementers with no guidance on what to build or how to verify the bound.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a sentence such as: 'To prevent thrash under high churn, each stack is retried at most N times per batch window (exact limit TBD by implementer, must be documented); stacks exceeding the limit are parked and their owning agent notified.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the retry bound explicitly (numeric value or formula), document how it was derived (or mark it 'TBD: derive from observed flap rates'), and make it a configurable parameter rather than a hardcoded constant.
findings.finding: 'Bounded retries prevent thrash' is asserted without specifying the bound, how it was derived, or whether it is configurable. Retry limits under churn are expected to evolve as real workload patterns emerge.
findings.location: AC line 3 — 'bounded retries prevent thrash under churn'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concurrency/ownership contract for batch state during attribution: specify whether innocent-stack HEADs are snapshotted atomically at failure-detection time, and what mechanism (lock, CAS, transaction) serializes batch-state mutations.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomic ownership of the combined batch's shared state during failure attribution: between culprit identification and innocent re-batching, concurrent commits to innocent stacks (pushed by their agents) could invalidate the re-batch snapshot, and no locking, CAS, or transaction boundary is described.
findings.location: What / AC1 and AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the multi-culprit case: when K > 1 stacks fail in a combined batch run, all K culprit stacks are returned to their agents and the remaining (N-K) innocent stacks are re-batched (or state that K=1 is an invariant of the batching-assembly phase this story depends on).
findings.finding: AC1's framing is singular ('the culprit stack'), but a combined batch can have multiple failing stacks simultaneously. The plan does not specify what happens when K > 1 stacks fail: are all K stacks handed back to their agents and the remaining N-K re-batched, or does the plan assume at most one failing stack per combined run?
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1 and AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC1 (or the What section) the fallback behavior when the isolated culprit-stack CI re-run is inconclusive (timeout, infrastructure failure, or cancellation): e.g., treat as transient and retry up to the bounded-retry limit, or park the stack and surface via the outcome contract.
findings.finding: AC1 requires 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct Verified -1' but specifies no fallback for the case where this isolated re-run is itself inconclusive — e.g., CI times out, infrastructure fails, or the run is cancelled. The sub-step has no defined fallback outcome.
findings.location: AC1: 're-run CI on the isolated culprit stack to produce a direct `Verified -1`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule for the multi-culprit case: specify what happens when attribution identifies more than one culprit stack (e.g., isolate and re-CI each independently in parallel, sequentially, or treat the entire batch as failed and hand all stacks back).
findings.finding: The plan addresses only the singular-culprit case ('the culprit stack', 'its owning agent') and provides no decision rule or fallback for the case where multiple stacks in a batch are independently culpable. This is an unspecified fallback for an incomplete sub-step branching path that an executor will encounter.
findings.location: AC1–AC2: singular framing 'the culprit stack' / 'its owning agent'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retirement/cleanup path for failed batch records and specify idempotency semantics for the attribution flow (e.g., re-entrant: re-running attribution on an already-attributed batch is safe and produces the same outcome).
findings.finding: The failed combined batch record has no defined retirement or cleanup path, and the plan does not address idempotency if the attribution-and-re-batch operation is interrupted and retried mid-way.
findings.location: What / AC1, AC2 — whole plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: emit a structured log/metric/event for each attribution decision (batch-id, culprit-stack-id, innocent-stack-ids, re-run result) and alert on attribution failure or re-batch failure.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the failure-attribution process: which stack was identified as culprit, why, and the outcome of the isolated re-run and re-batch are not required to be logged, metricked, or alerted on.
findings.location: What / Acceptance Criteria (whole plan)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test specifications (or explicit AC sub-items) covering: (a) ambiguous/multi-stack failure attribution, (b) culprit re-CI failure or timeout, (c) innocent re-batch cascading failure, (d) single-stack batch (no innocents), and (e) retry-limit exhaustion — each with an observable, non-tautological outcome.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new multi-branch failure-handling flow (culprit attribution, isolated re-CI, innocent re-batching, bounded retries) but specifies NO test coverage — no unit, integration, or scenario-level tests — and in particular omits failure/edge-path scenarios: ambiguous attribution (multiple stacks fail simultaneously), culprit re-CI itself failing or timing out, innocent re-batch also failing, a batch where every stack is a culprit (no innocents), and retry-limit exhaustion behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs) and 'What' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Test scenarios' sub-section to the plan (or annotate each AC) that lists: (1) unit tests for the attribution algorithm with ≥2 stacks failing simultaneously; (2) an integration test where isolated re-CI returns failure — assert the stack gets `ci_failed` and retries decrement; (3) a test where retry limit N is reached — assert the stack is parked/dead-lettered rather than looping; (4) a test for a single-stack batch — assert no re-batch is attempted. Each assertion should be expressed in terms of the external contract (outcome label, queue state) not internal implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the stack-granular attribution decision, the bounded-retry policy, and the reuse of the serial `needs_rebase`/`ci_failed` contract as the canonical record for this pipeline stage.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel pipeline stage (batch-failure attribution + re-batch policy) and a new behavioural contract that crosses the batch orchestrator and agent-facing failure components, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry is mentioned.
findings.location: 'What' and 'Scope' sections; AC lines 1–3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the chosen approach (e.g., 're-batching innocents preserves the throughput benefits of combined CI for the majority case while the culprit pays the serial penalty; this is preferred over a full serial fallback because [reason]').
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing the 'isolate-culprit + re-batch-innocents' approach over structurally different alternatives (e.g., always decompose batch failures into per-stack serial CI runs, which eliminates the attribution problem at the cost of throughput). The 'Why' section motivates the requirement but not the design choice.
findings.location: Why / What / Scope sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing S9's culprit-attribution algorithm and re-batching strategy, review and document how Bors-ng, Zuul's pipeline failure handling, and GitHub Merge Queue's group failure attribution address the same problem (flaky-test misattribution, multi-culprit batches, ordering-dependent innocents), and record which approach S9 adopts and why.
findings.finding: The plan's combined-batch failure attribution + re-batching-of-innocents pattern is exactly the problem Zuul, Bors-ng, GitHub Merge Queue, and GitLab Merge Trains solve, but none of these are named or consulted. ADR-0040 rejected Zuul by name on cost/ops grounds, but the rejection was about the full control plane — not about the attribution algorithm. The specific sub-problem S9 is implementing (how to identify which stack in a combined run caused CI failure, and how to re-queue the rest) is a well-studied domain with documented edge cases (flaky tests misattributing blame, multi-culprit batches, ordering dependencies between 'innocent' stacks). Committing to a bespoke design without grounding in these existing solutions risks reinventing failure modes they evolved to handle.
findings.location: What / AC1 ('attribute the culprit at STACK granularity') and AC2 ('re-batch the innocent stacks into a fresh combined run')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
